[[ja:%E4%BB%99%E5%8F%B0%E7%99%BD%E8%8F%9C]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:redirect]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]]

[[ja:%E4%BB%99%E5%8F%B0%E7%99%BD%E8%8F%9C]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:redirect]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Chinese cabbage|ja|ハクサイ}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

[[ja:%E4%BB%99%E5%8F%B0%E7%99%BD%E8%8F%9C]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:redirect]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Chinese cabbage|ja|ハクサイ}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

[[ja:%E4%BB%99%E5%8F%B0%E7%99%BD%E8%8F%9C]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] [[en:Sendai Hakusai]] == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==

It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>

The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>

By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>

The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Chinese cabbage|ja|ハクサイ|12=draft}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争|12=draft}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団|12=draft}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=draft}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜|12=draft}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=draft}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=draft}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区|12=draft}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|12=draft}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=draft}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=draft}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|12=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|12=draft}} * {{ill|Crop rotation|ja|連作|12=draft}} * {{ill|Crossbreeding|ja|交雑|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|ja|長町駅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|12=draft}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
{{citation needed|Sendai Hakusai}}

However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|12=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=draft}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区|12=draft}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|12=draft}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=draft}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=draft}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|12=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|12=draft}} * {{ill|Crop rotation|ja|連作|12=draft}} * {{ill|Crossbreeding|ja|交雑|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|ja|長町駅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|12=draft}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
{{citation needed|Sendai Hakusai}}

revision.text:
== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Chinese cabbage|ja|ハクサイ|12=draft|12=draft}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft|12=draft}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争|12=draft|12=draft}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団|12=draft|12=draft}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=draft|12=draft}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=draft|12=draft}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=draft|12=draft}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|12=draft|12=draft}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=draft|12=draft}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|12=draft|12=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Crop rotation|ja|連作|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Crossbreeding|ja|交雑|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|ja|長町駅|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|12=draft|12=draft}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
{{citation needed|Sendai Hakusai}}

The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|qq=draft|qq=draft}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>

The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Crop rotation|ja|連作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Crossbreeding|ja|交雑|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|ja|長町駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.comment: 41 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:仙台白菜 up to rev 102030283
revision.text:
'''仙台白菜'''（せんだいはくさい）は、[[日本]]の[[宮城県]]で生まれ、同地を主産地とする[[ハクサイ|白菜]]の[[品種]]群である。[[中国]]の[[白菜]]を元に[[1920年]]（[[大正]]9年）頃に作り出された品種「'''松島白菜'''」（まつしまはくさい）と、さらに[[品種改良]]された「'''松島純二号」'''（まつしまじゅんにごう）、「'''松島新二号」'''（まつしましんにごう）などの総称、および、[[商品]]名として用いられる。
[[20世紀]]半ばまで全国に送り出されたが、現在は[[仙台市]]など地元を中心に消費されている。出荷時期は、11月上旬から12月下旬<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm 仙台地域の食材情報]（宮城県・仙台地方振興事務所）</ref>。 {{座標一覧}}
== 歴史 == 仙台白菜の起源は、[[日露戦争]]（[[1904年]]（[[明治]]37年）[[2月8日]] - [[1905年]]（明治38年）[[9月5日]]）に出征した[[第2師団 (日本軍)|第2師団]]（[[衛戍|衛戍地]]：仙台市）の兵士<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html 仙台白菜の生みの親 沼倉吉兵衛（ぬまくらきちべえ）]（[http://taihakumachikyo.org/ 太白区まちづくり推進協議会]）</ref>・庄司金兵衛が、[[中国東北部]]で[[ハクサイ|白菜]]（おもに[[酸菜]]に使われる「[[:zh:大白菜|大白菜]]」）の[[種子]]を得て郷里の友人に配ったことにあると言われる<ref name="Kinjiro">山本金次郎・編『宮城県名勝地誌』251-252頁</ref>。育てられた白菜は好評だったが、採取した種子が親の性質を引き継がないという障害があった。
これを品種として確立させたのは、宮城県農学校（当時の[[名取郡]][[茂ヶ崎村]]、現・仙台市[[太白区]]、{{ウィキ座標|38|14|17.5|N|140|53|7.9|E|region:JP|地図|name=宮城県農学校（跡地は宮城県武道館ほか）}}）<ref name="MiyagiAgri" group="†">後に[[宮城県農業高等学校]]となり、[[名取市]]に移転。宮城県農学校に隣接する実習用地は[[宮城県農業短期大学]]（現在は[[宮城大学]]食産業学部に継承）に転用された後、現在は[[宮城県仙台南高等学校]]などになっている。</ref>と伊達家養種園（当時の仙台市、現・仙台市[[若林区]]、{{ウィキ座標|38|14|39|N|140|54|2.7|E|region:JP|地図|name=伊達家養種園（跡地は若林区役所）}}）<ref group="†">後に移転して[[せんだい農業園芸センター]]となった。</ref>に勤務した[[沼倉吉兵衛]]<ref group="†">[[1859年]]（[[安政]]6年）、現在の宮城県[[登米市]][[登米町]]生まれ。[[1943年]]（昭和18年）逝去。</ref>であった<ref name="taihk185"/>。白菜が他の[[アブラナ科]]の植物と[[交雑]]しやすいのが原因であったため、沼倉は[[松島湾]]の[[馬放島]]（当時の[[宮城郡]][[浦戸村]]、現・[[塩竈市]]、{{ウィキ座標|38|19|34.4|N|141|4|12.8|E|region:JP|地図|name=馬放島}}）<ref group="†">[[浦戸諸島]]の[[無人島]]の1つ。</ref>で隔離栽培して種子を得た。その後、松島白菜をもとに、(株)[https://watanabe-seed.com/ 渡辺採種場]の創業者渡辺穎二が改良を進めて「松島純一号」を採種し、さらに[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）には「松島純二号」などの改良種が生まれた<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html 【宮城】仙台白菜、普及へ「産官学民」連携]（[[朝日新聞]] 2011年12月3日）。</ref>。くだって[[1943年]]（昭和18年）には「松島新二号」が生まれた<ref name="asahi"/>。それらの種子を宮城県農会を通じて県内の農家に配り、栽培させた。県内では特に名取郡で栽培が盛んであった。
[[1921年]]（大正10年）に名取郡[[長町青物市場]]（{{Coord|38|14|4.5|N|140|53|14.7|E|region:JP|name=長町青物市場（跡地はパシフィックマンション広瀬橋）}}）<ref group="†">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk231/index.html 長町青果市場]（太白区まちづくり推進協議会）… [[1892年]]（明治25年）から[[1963年]]（昭和38年）まで開場した。</ref><ref group="†">[http://www.mazarain.com/sanpop20.html 歴史散歩 ぶらり長町 ポイント20：長町青果市場跡]（長町まざらいん）… 長町青物市場の跡地はパシフィックマンション広瀬橋となっている。</ref>の阿部太平が初めて[[東京都中央卸売市場|東京市場]]に出荷した<ref name="Kinjiro"/>。[[1923年]]（大正12年）[[9月1日]]に[[大正関東地震]]（[[関東大震災]]）が発生すると、甚大な被害を受けた[[東京府]]（現・[[東京都]]）に向けて宮城県農会が鉄道[[貨車]]7両分の白菜を送り出した。ところで、それまで東京において[[漬け菜]]の主流は[[三河島菜]]だったが、当時の主産地である東京府[[北豊島郡]][[尾久町]]（現・東京都[[荒川区]]）周辺に、同地震で甚大な被害を受けた東京府[[東京市]]から移住者が殺到して急速に[[市街地]]化が進行した<ref>[http://www.tokyo-aff.or.jp/center/chishiki/05/saimituga_vasp.htm 細密画]（[[公益財団法人]]東京都農林水産振興財団）</ref><ref>[http://www.yasaitobunka.or.jp/yasai_school/2012/20120407_edo/katsudo20120407_edo-004.html ◆三河島菜 ＜アブラナ科＞]（NPO法人野菜と文化のフォーラム）</ref><ref name="ArakawaWardHistory">{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.arakawa.tokyo.jp/kusei/gaiyo/guidemap/guidemap.files/rekishi.PDF 歴史]}}（荒川区）</ref><ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.waseda.jp/sem-muranolt01/Survey2002/ch3.pdf 第三章 荒川区]}}（[[早稲田大学]]「まちづくり活動の実態と可能性--足立区・荒川区・台東区・葛飾区の現状と課題」）</ref>。すると、三河島菜の栽培地が宅地に取って代わられて減少し、その一方で仙台白菜が東京に大量移入される事態が同期して、漬け菜の主流は三河島菜から白菜へと変化した<ref name="NHK">[http://www.nhk.or.jp/kaisetsu-blog/400/81883.html 物語で伝える"伝統野菜"]（[[日本放送協会|NHK]]『[[視点・論点]]』 2011年05月06日）</ref>。仙台白菜は全国的に普及し、[[1932年]]（[[昭和]]7年）には貨車5068両で161万6353[[俵 (単位)|俵]]が県外に送り出された<ref name="SendaiHistory24">『せんだい市史通信』24号</ref>。
仙台白菜の全盛期は[[第2次世界大戦]]の前までである。戦時中に交雑のせいで品種が劣化し、復活は[[連合国軍占領下の日本|戦後占領期]]の[[1951年]]（昭和26年）になった。その頃には他の品種が登場し、仙台白菜は栽培が難しい、柔らかくて傷がつきやすいといった理由で敬遠され、市場から消えてしまった。
[[1959年]]（[[昭和]]34年）、[[宮城県農業高等学校]]（旧・宮城県農学校）<ref name="MiyagiAgri" group="†"/>の用地内にある[[兜塚古墳 (仙台市)|兜塚古墳]]の頂上に、農学校の同窓会によって沼倉吉兵衛の胸像が設置された（{{Coord|38|14|19.2|N|140|53|3.4|E|region:JP|name=兜塚古墳。頂上に沼倉吉兵衛の胸像あり。}}）。
[[2001年]]（平成13年）の「仙台開府400年」の様々な事業を執り行った仙台開府四百年記念事業推進協議会（会長：仙台商工会議所会頭）が、開府500年に向けた「仙台ブランドの創出」を目標として活動を開始し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）10月に「仙台ブランド推進委員会」を設立した<ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.sendaicci.or.jp/1machi/brand/brand.pdf 仙台ブランド推進委員会]}}（仙台商工会議所 2004年3月24日）</ref>。この流れに呼応する形で、県や仙台市農業団体などが「仙台野菜ブランド化推進協議会」を構成し、[[2004年]]（平成16年）[[12月22日]]に「[[仙台伝統野菜]]フォーラム」を開催した<ref>[http://www.jlp.net/letter/050205b.html 衰退する仙台市近郊の野菜畑]（[[労働新聞 (日本労働党)|労働新聞]] 2005年2月5日号）{{リンク切れ|date=2024年9月}}</ref>。これ以降、仙台伝統野菜の生産・[[流通]]・消費・[[食育]]などの具体的な取り組みが徐々に行われ、仙台白菜も[[伝統野菜]]の1つとして見直された<ref name="SendaiHistory24"/>。[[2010年]]（[[平成]]22年）からは、[[仙台大学附属明成高等学校|明成高等学校]]（仙台市）が栽培や商品開発などに取り組み始め、普及に一役買うことになった<ref name="asahi"/><ref>[http://www.kahoku.co.jp/news/2011/06/20110630t12019.htm 「仙台白菜」ブランド化 津波被害の農家支援]（[[河北新報]] 2010年6月30日）</ref>。また、[[塩害]]に強いとされるため、[[2011年]]（平成23年）[[3月11日]]に発生した[[東北地方太平洋沖地震]]（[[東日本大震災]]）による[[津波]]で[[海水]]を被った[[農地]]において、栽培プロジェクトが始まっている<ref>[http://jyoho.kahoku.co.jp/member/backnum/news/2011/08/20110817t13018.htm 戦前の「仙台白菜荷札」発見 石巻・桃生の民家]（河北新報 2011年8月17日）</ref><ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/press/pdf/110624-2.pdf ～『仙台白菜』で宮城の農業の復興を目指します～ 「みんなの新しいふるさとづくりプロジェクト」発足の報告会について]}}（宮城県 2011年6月24日）</ref>。
なお、仙台白菜に駆逐された形となった三河島菜（在来種の青茎および変種の白茎の2種）は[[絶滅]]したと考えられていたが、[[江戸時代]]に[[仙台藩]]の[[足軽]]が[[参勤交代]]の際に[[江戸]]（現・東京都）から地元に持ち帰り、現在でも宮城県内で栽培され、[[仙台朝市]]などで販売されていることが2010年（平成22年）に判明した<ref name="NHK"/><ref name="JANP">「三河島菜」ロマン『仙台から"里帰り" 食育組み合わせ復活』（[[日本農業新聞]]「地域発 農のデザイン」） </ref><ref name="EdoYasaiBlog40717235">[http://edoyasai.sblo.jp/article/40717235.html 仙台で伝統野菜談義、三河島菜のその後]（江戸東京・伝統野菜研究会「江戸東京野菜通信」 2010年9月15日）</ref>。それが仙台伝統野菜の1つ「[[仙台芭蕉菜]]」であり、在来種である青茎の三河島菜にあたる<ref name="NHK"/><ref name="JANP"/><ref name="EdoYasaiBlog40717235"/>。同年から東京都[[小平市]]で栽培が開始され、2011年（平成23年）からはかつての主産地だった荒川区の小学校でも食育の一環として栽培されている<ref name="NHK"/><ref name="JANP"/><ref name="EdoYasaiBlog40717235"/>。すなわち、[[江戸野菜]]「三河島菜」<ref>[http://www.edotokyo-yasai.com/index.html 江戸東京野菜普及推進連絡協議会]</ref>と仙台伝統野菜「仙台芭蕉菜」は同じものであり、各々地で伝統野菜の1つとしても認定されていることになる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="†"|}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}
=== 参考文献 === * 斎藤富「仙台白菜」、山本金次郎・編『宮城県名勝地誌』、宮城県教育会、1931年。 * 『せんだい市史通信』第24号、2011年。
== 関連項目 == * [[伝統野菜]] * [[連作]] * [[交雑]] * [[長町駅]] * [[仙台朝市]]（仙台伝統野菜の普及活動をしている。仙台芭蕉菜が三河島菜として「里帰り」するきっかけを作った）
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm 仙台伝統野菜のご紹介]（宮城県・仙台地方振興事務所） * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html 『仙台白菜』とともに地域の復興と活性化を目指す] - [[農林水産省]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[仙台白菜]] to [[Sendai Hakusai]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''仙台白菜'''（せんだいはくさい）は、[[日本]]の[[宮城県]]で生まれ、同地を主産地とする[[ハクサイ|白菜]]の[[品種]]群である。[[中国]]の[[白菜]]を元に[[1920年]]（[[大正]]9年）頃に作り出された品種「'''松島白菜'''」（まつしまはくさい）と、さらに[[品種改良]]された「'''松島純二号」'''（まつしまじゅんにごう）、「'''松島新二号」'''（まつしましんにごう）などの総称、および、[[商品]]名として用いられる。
[[20世紀]]半ばまで全国に送り出されたが、現在は[[仙台市]]など地元を中心に消費されている。出荷時期は、11月上旬から12月下旬<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm 仙台地域の食材情報]（宮城県・仙台地方振興事務所）</ref>。 {{座標一覧}}
== 歴史 == 仙台白菜の起源は、[[日露戦争]]（[[1904年]]（[[明治]]37年）[[2月8日]] - [[1905年]]（明治38年）[[9月5日]]）に出征した[[第2師団 (日本軍)|第2師団]]（[[衛戍|衛戍地]]：仙台市）の兵士<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html 仙台白菜の生みの親 沼倉吉兵衛（ぬまくらきちべえ）]（[http://taihakumachikyo.org/ 太白区まちづくり推進協議会]）</ref>・庄司金兵衛が、[[中国東北部]]で[[ハクサイ|白菜]]（おもに[[酸菜]]に使われる「[[:zh:大白菜|大白菜]]」）の[[種子]]を得て郷里の友人に配ったことにあると言われる<ref name="Kinjiro">山本金次郎・編『宮城県名勝地誌』251-252頁</ref>。育てられた白菜は好評だったが、採取した種子が親の性質を引き継がないという障害があった。
これを品種として確立させたのは、宮城県農学校（当時の[[名取郡]][[茂ヶ崎村]]、現・仙台市[[太白区]]、{{ウィキ座標|38|14|17.5|N|140|53|7.9|E|region:JP|地図|name=宮城県農学校（跡地は宮城県武道館ほか）}}）<ref name="MiyagiAgri" group="†">後に[[宮城県農業高等学校]]となり、[[名取市]]に移転。宮城県農学校に隣接する実習用地は[[宮城県農業短期大学]]（現在は[[宮城大学]]食産業学部に継承）に転用された後、現在は[[宮城県仙台南高等学校]]などになっている。</ref>と伊達家養種園（当時の仙台市、現・仙台市[[若林区]]、{{ウィキ座標|38|14|39|N|140|54|2.7|E|region:JP|地図|name=伊達家養種園（跡地は若林区役所）}}）<ref group="†">後に移転して[[せんだい農業園芸センター]]となった。</ref>に勤務した[[沼倉吉兵衛]]<ref group="†">[[1859年]]（[[安政]]6年）、現在の宮城県[[登米市]][[登米町]]生まれ。[[1943年]]（昭和18年）逝去。</ref>であった<ref name="taihk185"/>。白菜が他の[[アブラナ科]]の植物と[[交雑]]しやすいのが原因であったため、沼倉は[[松島湾]]の[[馬放島]]（当時の[[宮城郡]][[浦戸村]]、現・[[塩竈市]]、{{ウィキ座標|38|19|34.4|N|141|4|12.8|E|region:JP|地図|name=馬放島}}）<ref group="†">[[浦戸諸島]]の[[無人島]]の1つ。</ref>で隔離栽培して種子を得た。その後、松島白菜をもとに、(株)[https://watanabe-seed.com/ 渡辺採種場]の創業者渡辺穎二が改良を進めて「松島純一号」を採種し、さらに[[1924年]]（[[大正]]13年）には「松島純二号」などの改良種が生まれた<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html 【宮城】仙台白菜、普及へ「産官学民」連携]（[[朝日新聞]] 2011年12月3日）。</ref>。くだって[[1943年]]（昭和18年）には「松島新二号」が生まれた<ref name="asahi"/>。それらの種子を宮城県農会を通じて県内の農家に配り、栽培させた。県内では特に名取郡で栽培が盛んであった。
[[1921年]]（大正10年）に名取郡[[長町青物市場]]（{{Coord|38|14|4.5|N|140|53|14.7|E|region:JP|name=長町青物市場（跡地はパシフィックマンション広瀬橋）}}）<ref group="†">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk231/index.html 長町青果市場]（太白区まちづくり推進協議会）… [[1892年]]（明治25年）から[[1963年]]（昭和38年）まで開場した。</ref><ref group="†">[http://www.mazarain.com/sanpop20.html 歴史散歩 ぶらり長町 ポイント20：長町青果市場跡]（長町まざらいん）… 長町青物市場の跡地はパシフィックマンション広瀬橋となっている。</ref>の阿部太平が初めて[[東京都中央卸売市場|東京市場]]に出荷した<ref name="Kinjiro"/>。[[1923年]]（大正12年）[[9月1日]]に[[大正関東地震]]（[[関東大震災]]）が発生すると、甚大な被害を受けた[[東京府]]（現・[[東京都]]）に向けて宮城県農会が鉄道[[貨車]]7両分の白菜を送り出した。ところで、それまで東京において[[漬け菜]]の主流は[[三河島菜]]だったが、当時の主産地である東京府[[北豊島郡]][[尾久町]]（現・東京都[[荒川区]]）周辺に、同地震で甚大な被害を受けた東京府[[東京市]]から移住者が殺到して急速に[[市街地]]化が進行した<ref>[http://www.tokyo-aff.or.jp/center/chishiki/05/saimituga_vasp.htm 細密画]（[[公益財団法人]]東京都農林水産振興財団）</ref><ref>[http://www.yasaitobunka.or.jp/yasai_school/2012/20120407_edo/katsudo20120407_edo-004.html ◆三河島菜 ＜アブラナ科＞]（NPO法人野菜と文化のフォーラム）</ref><ref name="ArakawaWardHistory">{{PDFlink|[http://www.city.arakawa.tokyo.jp/kusei/gaiyo/guidemap/guidemap.files/rekishi.PDF 歴史]}}（荒川区）</ref><ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.waseda.jp/sem-muranolt01/Survey2002/ch3.pdf 第三章 荒川区]}}（[[早稲田大学]]「まちづくり活動の実態と可能性--足立区・荒川区・台東区・葛飾区の現状と課題」）</ref>。すると、三河島菜の栽培地が宅地に取って代わられて減少し、その一方で仙台白菜が東京に大量移入される事態が同期して、漬け菜の主流は三河島菜から白菜へと変化した<ref name="NHK">[http://www.nhk.or.jp/kaisetsu-blog/400/81883.html 物語で伝える"伝統野菜"]（[[日本放送協会|NHK]]『[[視点・論点]]』 2011年05月06日）</ref>。仙台白菜は全国的に普及し、[[1932年]]（[[昭和]]7年）には貨車5068両で161万6353[[俵 (単位)|俵]]が県外に送り出された<ref name="SendaiHistory24">『せんだい市史通信』24号</ref>。
仙台白菜の全盛期は[[第2次世界大戦]]の前までである。戦時中に交雑のせいで品種が劣化し、復活は[[連合国軍占領下の日本|戦後占領期]]の[[1951年]]（昭和26年）になった。その頃には他の品種が登場し、仙台白菜は栽培が難しい、柔らかくて傷がつきやすいといった理由で敬遠され、市場から消えてしまった。
[[1959年]]（[[昭和]]34年）、[[宮城県農業高等学校]]（旧・宮城県農学校）<ref name="MiyagiAgri" group="†"/>の用地内にある[[兜塚古墳 (仙台市)|兜塚古墳]]の頂上に、農学校の同窓会によって沼倉吉兵衛の胸像が設置された（{{Coord|38|14|19.2|N|140|53|3.4|E|region:JP|name=兜塚古墳。頂上に沼倉吉兵衛の胸像あり。}}）。
[[2001年]]（平成13年）の「仙台開府400年」の様々な事業を執り行った仙台開府四百年記念事業推進協議会（会長：仙台商工会議所会頭）が、開府500年に向けた「仙台ブランドの創出」を目標として活動を開始し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）10月に「仙台ブランド推進委員会」を設立した<ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.sendaicci.or.jp/1machi/brand/brand.pdf 仙台ブランド推進委員会]}}（仙台商工会議所 2004年3月24日）</ref>。この流れに呼応する形で、県や仙台市農業団体などが「仙台野菜ブランド化推進協議会」を構成し、[[2004年]]（平成16年）[[12月22日]]に「[[仙台伝統野菜]]フォーラム」を開催した<ref>[http://www.jlp.net/letter/050205b.html 衰退する仙台市近郊の野菜畑]（[[労働新聞 (日本労働党)|労働新聞]] 2005年2月5日号）{{リンク切れ|date=2024年9月}}</ref>。これ以降、仙台伝統野菜の生産・[[流通]]・消費・[[食育]]などの具体的な取り組みが徐々に行われ、仙台白菜も[[伝統野菜]]の1つとして見直された<ref name="SendaiHistory24"/>。[[2010年]]（[[平成]]22年）からは、[[仙台大学附属明成高等学校|明成高等学校]]（仙台市）が栽培や商品開発などに取り組み始め、普及に一役買うことになった<ref name="asahi"/><ref>[http://www.kahoku.co.jp/news/2011/06/20110630t12019.htm 「仙台白菜」ブランド化 津波被害の農家支援]（[[河北新報]] 2010年6月30日）</ref>。また、[[塩害]]に強いとされるため、[[2011年]]（平成23年）[[3月11日]]に発生した[[東北地方太平洋沖地震]]（[[東日本大震災]]）による[[津波]]で[[海水]]を被った[[農地]]において、栽培プロジェクトが始まっている<ref>[http://jyoho.kahoku.co.jp/member/backnum/news/2011/08/20110817t13018.htm 戦前の「仙台白菜荷札」発見 石巻・桃生の民家]（河北新報 2011年8月17日）</ref><ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/press/pdf/110624-2.pdf ～『仙台白菜』で宮城の農業の復興を目指します～ 「みんなの新しいふるさとづくりプロジェクト」発足の報告会について]}}（宮城県 2011年6月24日）</ref>。
なお、仙台白菜に駆逐された形となった三河島菜（在来種の青茎および変種の白茎の2種）は[[絶滅]]したと考えられていたが、[[江戸時代]]に[[仙台藩]]の[[足軽]]が[[参勤交代]]の際に[[江戸]]（現・東京都）から地元に持ち帰り、現在でも宮城県内で栽培され、[[仙台朝市]]などで販売されていることが2010年（平成22年）に判明した<ref name="NHK"/><ref name="JANP">「三河島菜」ロマン『仙台から"里帰り" 食育組み合わせ復活』（[[日本農業新聞]]「地域発 農のデザイン」） </ref><ref name="EdoYasaiBlog40717235">[http://edoyasai.sblo.jp/article/40717235.html 仙台で伝統野菜談義、三河島菜のその後]（江戸東京・伝統野菜研究会「江戸東京野菜通信」 2010年9月15日）</ref>。それが仙台伝統野菜の1つ「[[仙台芭蕉菜]]」であり、在来種である青茎の三河島菜にあたる<ref name="NHK"/><ref name="JANP"/><ref name="EdoYasaiBlog40717235"/>。同年から東京都[[小平市]]で栽培が開始され、2011年（平成23年）からはかつての主産地だった荒川区の小学校でも食育の一環として栽培されている<ref name="NHK"/><ref name="JANP"/><ref name="EdoYasaiBlog40717235"/>。すなわち、[[江戸野菜]]「三河島菜」<ref>[http://www.edotokyo-yasai.com/index.html 江戸東京野菜普及推進連絡協議会]</ref>と仙台伝統野菜「仙台芭蕉菜」は同じものであり、各々地で伝統野菜の1つとしても認定されていることになる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="†"|}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}

== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Chinese cabbage|ja|ハクサイ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|qq=draft|qq=draft}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Crop rotation|ja|連作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Crossbreeding|ja|交雑|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|ja|長町駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)

This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Chinese cabbage|ja|ハクサイ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|ja|仙台市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|ja|第2師団|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|ja|酸菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku Ward|ja|太白区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi Ward|ja|若林区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|qq=draft|qq=draft}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Crop rotation|ja|連作|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Crossbreeding|ja|交雑|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|ja|長町駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links ==

Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|ja|アブラナ科|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Brassicaceae|qq=}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Meisei High School|ja|仙台大学附属明成高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisei High School|qq=}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.

The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

revision.text:
== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|1=Japanese Traditional Vegetables|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Traditional vegetables|qq=}} * [[:en:Monocropping|Crop rotation]] * [[:en:Crossbreed|Crossbreeding]] * [[:en:Nagamachi Station|Nagamachi Station]] * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Morning Market|qq=}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
{{translated page|ja|仙台白菜|version=102030283|comment=Imported full ja history}}

This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>

However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[:en:1920|1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|1=Japanese Traditional Vegetables|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Traditional vegetables|qq=}} * [[:en:Monocropping|Crop rotation]] * [[:en:Crossbreed|Crossbreeding]] * [[:en:Nagamachi Station|Nagamachi Station]] * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Morning Market|qq=}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
{{translated page|ja|仙台白菜|version=102030283|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 1]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11378039]] Wikidata name: Sendai hakusai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[:en:1920|1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==

== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
