{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11378039]] Wikidata name: Sendai hakusai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[:en:1920|1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11378039]] Wikidata name: Sendai hakusai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[:en:1920|1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|1=traditional vegetable|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Traditional vegetables|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381421}} * [[:en:Monocropping|Crop rotation]] * [[:en:Crossbreed|Crossbreeding]] * [[:en:Nagamachi Station|Nagamachi Station]] * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Morning Market|qq=|1=Sendai Asaichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378013}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)

'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of [[:en:Napa cabbage|Chinese cabbage]] varieties that originated in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around [[:en:1920|1920]] (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as [[:en:Sendai|Sendai]]. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

== External Links ==

The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>

The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]] (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the [[:en:2nd Division|2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)]] stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for [[:en:Suan cai|Suan cai]], known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of [[:en:Taihaku-ku, Sendai|Taihaku Ward]], Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in [[:en:Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|Wakabayashi Ward]], Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other [[:en:Brassicaceae|Brassicaceae]] plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, [[:en:Sendai University Meisei High School|Meisei High School]] has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>

The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division|en|2nd Division|WD=Q209767|lt=2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|en|Suan cai|WD=Q3269753|lt=Suan cai}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|en|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1043331|lt=Taihaku Ward}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|en|Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1131743|lt=Wakabayashi Ward}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|en|Brassicaceae|WD=Q156888|lt=Brassicaceae}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Sendai University Meisei High School|en|Sendai University Meisei High School|WD=Q11512083|lt=Meisei High School}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:仙台白菜]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:仙台白菜]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:仙台白菜]]
== Sendai Chinese Cabbage ==
'''Sendai Chinese Cabbage''' (仙台白菜) is a group of {{ill|Napa cabbage|en|Napa cabbage|WD=Q13360268|lt=Chinese cabbage|1=Napa cabbage}} varieties that originated in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, and is primarily cultivated in the same region. It refers collectively to varieties such as "'''Matsushima Chinese Cabbage'''" (松島白菜), developed around {{ill|1920|en|1920|WD=Q2155|lt=1920|1=1920}} (Taisho 9), and further improved strains like "'''Matsushima Jun No. 2'''" (松島純二号) and "'''Matsushima Shin No. 2'''" (松島新二号), as well as a commercial product name.
Although it was distributed nationwide until the mid-20th century, today it is mainly consumed locally in areas such as {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|WD=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}}. The shipping season runs from early November to late December.<ref>[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sdsgsin/top/kegjoho/syokuzaijyouhou/vol2/syokuzaipr2.htm Information on Ingredients from Sendai Region] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
== History ==
The origin of Sendai Chinese Cabbage traces back to the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}} (February 8, 1904 – September 5, 1905), when a soldier from the {{ill|2nd Division|en|2nd Division|WD=Q209767|lt=2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|1=2nd Division}} stationed in Sendai, {{ill|Kinbei Shoji|ja|庄司金兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinbei Shoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, obtained seeds of Chinese cabbage (mainly used for {{ill|Suan cai|en|Suan cai|WD=Q3269753|lt=Suan cai|1=Suan cai}}, known as "{{ill|Napa cabbage|zh|大白菜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Napa cabbage|qq=}}") in Northeast China and distributed them to friends back home.<ref name="taihk185">[http://taihakumachikyo.org/taihk/taihk185/index.html The Creator of Sendai Chinese Cabbage: Kichibei Numakura] (Taihaku Ward Town Development Promotion Council)</ref> The cabbage cultivated from these seeds was well-received, but the seeds did not retain the parent plant's characteristics.
The variety was established thanks to the efforts of {{ill|Kichibei Numakura|ja|沼倉吉兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kichibei Numakura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked at Miyagi Prefectural Agricultural School (currently part of {{ill|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|en|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1043331|lt=Taihaku Ward|1=Taihaku-ku, Sendai}}, Sendai) and the Date Family Seed Garden (now in {{ill|Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|en|Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai|WD=Q1131743|lt=Wakabayashi Ward|1=Wakabayashi-ku, Sendai}}, Sendai).<ref name="MiyagiAgri">[http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture, Sendai Regional Promotion Office)</ref>
Due to the cabbage's tendency to crossbreed with other {{ill|Brassicaceae|en|Brassicaceae|WD=Q156888|lt=Brassicaceae|1=Brassicaceae}} plants, Numakura cultivated the seeds in isolation on {{ill|Umahanashima|ja|馬放島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umahanashima|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦戸諸島|1=Urato Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558262}}, an uninhabited island in {{ill|Matsushima Bay|ja|松島湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsushima Bay|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 松島|1=Matsushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1144855}}. This led to the development of "Matsushima Jun No. 1" and subsequent varieties like "Matsushima Jun No. 2" in 1924, followed by "Matsushima Shin No. 2" in 1943.<ref name="asahi">[https://archive.is/20130425002108/www.asahi.com/food/news/TKY201112030168.html Sendai Chinese Cabbage Revival through Collaboration] (Asahi Shimbun, December 3, 2011)</ref>
By 1932, Sendai Chinese Cabbage had spread nationwide, with 1,616,353 bales shipped out of the prefecture in 5,068 rail cars.<ref name="SendaiHistory24">Sendai City Historical Bulletin, Issue 24</ref>
== Decline and Revival ==
The peak of Sendai Chinese Cabbage production was before World War II. The variety deteriorated during the war due to crossbreeding. Its revival began in 1951 during the post-war occupation period. However, new varieties had emerged by then, and Sendai Chinese Cabbage was considered difficult to cultivate and fragile, leading to its disappearance from the market.
In 2001, Sendai's 400th-anniversary project reignited interest in traditional vegetables. The "Sendai Traditional Vegetables Forum" held in 2004 marked the beginning of systematic efforts to promote Sendai Chinese Cabbage. Since 2010, {{ill|Sendai University Meisei High School|en|Sendai University Meisei High School|WD=Q11512083|lt=Meisei High School|1=Sendai University Meisei High School}} has contributed to its cultivation and product development.<ref name="asahi"/>
== Related Items ==
* {{ill|Traditional vegetables|ja|伝統野菜|1=traditional vegetable|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Traditional vegetables|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381421}} * {{ill|Monocropping|en|Monocropping|WD=Q17146935|lt=Crop rotation|1=Monocropping}} * {{ill|Crossbreed|en|Crossbreed|WD=Q4390467|lt=Crossbreeding|1=Crossbreed}} * {{ill|Nagamachi Station|en|Nagamachi Station|WD=Q843033|lt=Nagamachi Station|1=Nagamachi Station}} * {{ill|Sendai Morning Market|ja|仙台朝市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Morning Market|1=Sendai Asaichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378013}}
== External Links ==
* [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/sd-nokai/sendaiyasaihp/shoukai.htm Introduction to Sendai Traditional Vegetables] (Miyagi Prefecture) * [https://www.maff.go.jp/j/pr/aff/1212/mf_news_00.html Reviving Sendai Agriculture with 'Sendai Chinese Cabbage'] (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries)
